Author:     Reimar Döffinger <Reimar.Doeffinger@gmx.de>

content.description: s->edge_emu_buffer = av_malloc(...) is called without checking whether the returned pointer is NULL. av_malloc() can fail and return NULL under low-memory conditions, and the unchecked NULL is stored and later dereferenced.

After the av_malloc() call, the fix adds a NULL check: if (!s->edge_emu_buffer) { ret = AVERROR(ENOMEM); goto error; }. This ensures the allocation failure is caught and the function returns cleanly instead of proceeding with a NULL buffer pointer.

The existing outer check 'if (!s->edge_emu_buffer)' (line 2682) guards the allocation to only execute when the buffer has not been previously allocated. This guard was present in both the vulnerable and fixed code and is not the root cause.

The missing condition was the absent NULL-check after av_malloc(). The vulnerable code had no 'if (!s->edge_emu_buffer)' failure-path guard between the allocation and the subsequent use of the pointer, making the null-pointer dereference reachable.

content.summary: Missing NULL-check on av_malloc() return value for s->edge_emu_buffer in vp3_decode_frame leads to NULL pointer dereference
content.mechanism: In vp3_decode_frame() at libavcodec/vp3.c:2682-2683 (pre-fix), the code checks whether s->edge_emu_buffer is NULL and conditionally allocates memory via av_malloc(), but never checks the return value. If av_malloc() fails (returns NULL), the NULL pointer is stored in s->edge_emu_buffer. Later at lines 2171 or 2185, the buffer pointer is assigned to a local 'temp' variable and dereferenced (via pointer arithmetic or passed to emulated_edge_mc / vp4_mc_loop_filter), causing a NULL pointer dereference / crash.
gate_errors: references evidence-gate-rejected anchor_id: ca-CVE-2022-3109-deref

content.description: The vulnerability requires av_malloc() to return NULL, which depends on system memory pressure; the exact trigger conditions are not reproducible from the evidence provided.

content.reason: The patch evidence clearly demonstrates the missing NULL-check, but no runtime crash reproduction or proof-of-concept was provided in the evidence to confirm the exploitability path.

content.description: parse_fmtp passes loop index i (position in rtsp_streams array) as the st_index argument to parse_sdp_a_line instead of using rtsp_st->stream_index (the AVStream index)

content.description: Changed argument from loop index i to rtsp_st->stream_index so parse_sdp_a_line receives the correct AVStream index for array lookups
RTSPStream has a stream_index field set to st->index (or -1) at initialization; the fix uses this correct field instead of the unrelated loop counter
gate_errors: predicate guard:CVE-2023-47342:001 references rejected anchor anchor:CVE-2023-47342:vuln:libavformat/rtsp.c:parse_fmtp

content.description: The problem is NOT a missing bounds check on i or on rt->nb_rtsp_streams; the loop bounds are correct. The problem is passing the wrong integer as st_index.
gate_errors: predicate neg:CVE-2023-47342:001 references rejected anchor anchor:CVE-2023-47342:vuln:libavformat/rtsp.c:parse_fmtp

content.summary: parse_fmtp passes loop index i instead of rtsp_st->stream_index to parse_sdp_a_line, causing out-of-bounds array access in downstream handlers
content.mechanism: In libavformat/rtsp.c, parse_fmtp iterates rt->rtsp_streams[] with loop counter i and passes i as the st_index argument to rtsp_st->dynamic_handler->parse_sdp_a_line(s, i, ...). The downstream callback (e.g. parse_h264_sdp_line in rtpdec_h264.c) uses this st_index to index s->streams[st_index]. Since i is the position in the rtsp_streams array and may differ from rtsp_st->stream_index (the AVStream index), this causes an out-of-bounds read on s->streams[].

content.pattern: return oprd->dims[0] * oprd->dims[1] * oprd->dims[2] * oprd->dims[3] * sizeof(float);
uint64_t len = sizeof(float); for (int i = 0; i < 4; i++) { len *= oprd->dims[i]; if (len > INT32_MAX) return 0; } return len;
NOTE: User must check for error (return value <= 0) to handle case like integer overflow.

content.description: The original calculate_operand_data_length performed dims[0]*dims[1]*dims[2]*dims[3]*sizeof(float) as a 32-bit int32 multiplication, which silently wraps on overflow.
After calculate_operand_data_length returns a potentially overflowed/wrapped value, callers passed it directly to av_realloc without validating that the length is positive and sensible.
gate_errors: predicate pred-vuln-missing-length-check has no shared GitObservation with anchor anchor-set-input-output

content.description: Changed the multiplication to use uint64_t intermediate, checking each step against INT32_MAX to detect overflow, returning 0 on overflow.

content.summary: Integer overflow in calculate_operand_data_length leads to undersized buffer allocation and subsequent heap buffer overflow in DNN native backend layer execution functions.
content.mechanism: The function calculate_operand_data_length multiplies four int32 dims and sizeof(float) using 32-bit arithmetic, which overflows to a small positive value when large dimensions are provided via a crafted DNN model. av_realloc then allocates a buffer smaller than expected, and subsequent layer write operations overflow the heap buffer.
